overview: This Pauper deck leverages the Madness mechanic to rapidly discard cards, triggering draw engines and direct damage spells. By cycling through the deck quickly, it aims to overwhelm opponents with early aggression and sustained card advantage. The strategy relies on efficient red removal and low-cost creatures to close games before opponents can stabilize.
win_conditions: Direct damage via Lightning Bolt and Lava Dart
Combat damage from Kessig Flamebreather and other red creatures
Hand disruption through repeated Madness triggers

keep_criteria: Hands with at least two one- or two-mana spells
Early Madness outlets to trigger draw effects
A mix of removal and creature threats
Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize duals or shocklands if available), at least one discard outlet or Madness enabler, and a turn-1 or turn-2 play (Lava Dart, Lightning Bolt, or a 1/2 drop).
Keep 2 lands if paired with a discard outlet + early threat or two cheap spells.
mulligan_criteria: Stuck on three-mana or higher cards
Mulligan on 4+ lands with no early plays, or 3+ lands with only removal and no threats/discard.

early_game: Play cheap creatures to establish board presence
Discard into Lightning Bolt or Lava Dart for immediate impact
Use Madness triggers to draw into follow-up threats
Turn 1: Play a land, then either play a 1-drop or discard a cheap spell with Madness to pressure or answer a threat.
Turn 2: Develop with a 2-drop or cast a discarded Madness spell for value.
Sequence mana to ensure R on T1, BR on T2, and UBR on T3.
Always prioritize casting discarded spells over letting them exile, and use discard to strip key threats or counterspells before they resolve.
mid_game: Maintain pressure while cycling through the deck
Prioritize discarding into high-value spells or removal
Block efficiently to protect key discard outlets
Treat your hand as a dynamic resource
discard strategically to fuel Madness rather than dumping randomly.
Preserve mana for interaction on opponent's turn, and remember that uncast Madness spells exile, so don't plan around graveyard recursion.
Use U mana for utility or protection when needed, but prioritize R/B for pressure and discard.
Commit threats when you have removal ready to answer their response or when they've exhausted interaction.
Hold back if they have sweepers, hard counters, or graveyard hate.
late_game: Leverage Kessig Flamebreather for sustained card advantage
Close out games with accumulated direct damage
Overwhelm opponent's resources with rapid card draw
Kessig Flamebreather as a resilient threat or burn finisher, chains of Lava Dart and Lightning Bolt for unblockable damage, or discarding a high-value Madness spell that resolves to swing the board.
Close games by forcing interaction until it's gone, then commit remaining resources.
Go all-in when the opponent has no interaction left, has low life total (≤10), or you've established board dominance with resilient threats.
Also commit when you can safely discard a high-impact Madness spell that they cannot counter or answer.

key_cards.name: Kessig Flamebreather
key_cards.role: Card advantage engine and primary attacker
key_cards.notes: Discard into this creature or discard other cards while it is on the battlefield to draw cards and maintain board presence.

key_cards.name: Lightning Bolt
key_cards.notes: Use the Madness cost to discard a card and deal three damage, bypassing blockers and accelerating the clock.

key_cards.name: Lava Dart
key_cards.notes: Cast early to trigger Madness effects, dealing two damage while cycling through the deck for better draws.

sequencing_tips: Hold up red mana to pay Madness costs when casting spells
Discard into removal only when necessary to preserve hand size
Prioritize discarding into Kessig Flamebreather to maximize draw potential
Use direct damage spells to finish games rather than overcommitting to combat
Common mistake: Discarding randomly without a plan.
Always ask if the discard fuels a Madness spell, removes a threat, or strips a key answer.
Kessig Flamebreather likely synergizes with discard or burn, so sequence it to maximize value.
Watch for format-specific hate cards and adjust your discard targets to protect your win conditions.

matchups.advice: Discard aggressively to deplete their hand and bypass counterspells
Race by playing creatures early and using direct damage to close gaps
Apply pressure before they stabilize with removal and draw

Use discard to remove their counterspells before they can be cast, or cast spells in response to their counterspells to manipulate the stack. Rely on cheap, uncounterable effects if available, and avoid overcommitting to expensive spells they can easily bounce or counter.

Apply maximum early pressure before they stabilize. Use removal to kill ramp creatures before they can cast big spells, and don't overextend into sweepers. Force them to use removal early, then close the game with discard and burn.

Win the discard war by keeping hands with multiple discard outlets and early threats. Trade removal efficiently, value out their discarded spells, and prioritize keeping life total high to outlast their late-game threats. Sideboard in targeted discard and cheap interaction to out-value them.

common_threats: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath, Terror), hard counters (e.g., Counterspell, Negate), and discard/graveyard hate (e.g., Rest in Peace, Leyline).
Note when opponents start drawing extra cards, have protection spells, or are holding up mana for multiple turns, as these signal a control or combo game plan.

overview_evidence.evidence_span: Mono Red Madness archetype in Pauper format utilizing Kessig Flamebreather, Lightning Bolt, and Lava Dart

win_conditions_evidence.evidence_span: Signature cards provide direct damage and discard-to-draw synergy

sequencing_tips_evidence.evidence_span: General Pauper red aggro sequencing principles applied to Madness mechanics

common_threats_evidence.evidence_span: Standard Pauper format threats that interact with red aggro strategies
